statement: If Iceland were to join the EU, it would need to adopt the full acquis communautaire. The EUR-Lex database records over 151,000 binding legal acts (regulations and directives combined) adopted since 1958, though many have been repealed or superseded. During Iceland's 2009–2015 accession negotiations, the screening process covered 33 chapters. Iceland had already adopted approximately one-third of the acquis through the EEA Agreement, principally in internal market areas, but would need to incorporate legislation in areas outside the EEA including fisheries policy (CFP), agriculture (CAP), monetary policy, and justice/home affairs.
caveats: The 'one-third of the acquis' figure is approximate and varies by counting methodology. The EEA acquis covers internal market legislation comprehensively but excludes several major policy areas. Many older acts in EUR-Lex are no longer in force.

related_entries.statement: The pace of EU legislative output has shifted markedly over the decades. In 1994–1998, the EU adopted an average of 2,907 regulations and 86 directives per year. By 2004–2008, this had fallen to 1,886 regulations and 112 directives per year. In the most recent period (2019–2023), annual output averaged 1,177 regulations and 48 directives. This declining trend in regulations reflects consolidation of the single market acquis, though recent years have seen major new legislative packages (Digital Services Act, AI Act, Green Deal regulations) that are individually more complex.

As of 2025, approximately 70% of EU internal market legislation has been incorporated into Icelandic law through the EEA Agreement. This covers free movement of goods, services, capital, and persons, but excludes fisheries, agriculture, and justice/home affairs.

claims.claim_text: Ríki sem hafa gengið í ESB hafi þurft að samþykkja alla hluta regluverksins.
